title: Resources
text: This class will not follow a textbook, but some of the methods and approaches may be new to students. For reference for machine learning methods, I recommend:

title: Resources
text:
Other Machine Learning References

Murphy, Kevin P. 2012. Machine Learning: A Probabilistic Perspective. Cambridge, MA: MIT Press.
Bishop, Christopher M. 2006. Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning. New York: Springer.

title: Resources
text:
Older Machine Learning References
Some older reference readings on core techniques in machine learning that are still relevant today. These are useful for students interested in the history and development of machine learning methods.

Breiman, Leo. 1996. “Bagging Predictors.” Machine Learning 24: 123–140.
Efron, Bradley. 1979. “Bootstrap Methods: Another Look at the Jackknife.” The Annals of Statistics 7(1): 1–26.
Breiman, Leo. 2001. “Random Forests.” Machine Learning 45: 5–32.

title: Resources
text:
A Causal/ML Textbook

Chernozhukov, Victor, Christian Hansen, Nathan Kallus, Martin Spindler, and Vasilis Syrgkanis. 2024. Applied Causal Inference Powered by ML and AI. arXiv.

title: Resources
text:
Causal Inference Background
Many of the papers we read in this course will use potential outcomes notation. Students who have not had recent coursework on causal inference should read the first few chapters of either:

Hernán, Miguel A., and James M. Robins. 2020. Causal Inference: What If. Boca Raton, FL: Chapman & Hall/CRC. A free pdf version of the book is available at the author’s website: link.
Imbens, Guido W., and Donald B. Rubin. 2015. Causal Inference in Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.

Also relevant:

Ding, Peng. 2023. A First Course in Causal Inference. arXiv.

As well, the materials for PLSC 30600: Causal Inference are available at this GitHub repository.

title: Resources
text:
Statistical Theory

Lehmann, E. L. 1999. Elements of Large-Sample Theory. New York: Springer.

title: Resources
text:
Econometrics

Angrist, Joshua D., and Jörn-Steffen Pischke. 2009. Mostly Harmless Econometrics: An Empiricist’s Companion. Princeton, NJ: Princeton University Press.

title: Projects

title: Projects
text:
What The Final Project Is
The final project is an original tutorial in which you explore a topic related to developments in machine learning for causal inference both analytically and computationally.
Briefly discuss the foundations for this work in statistics or related fields.
Demonstrate the method with a computational example by writing reproducible code.
The deliverable is a script or notebook and related data that can be run all the way through after setting the working directory.
The reader of your tutorial should come away with a good understanding of the strengths and drawbacks of the method or theme as well as some ideas about how to implement it.

title: Projects
text:
Publication Policy
Student work will only be posted publicly with the author’s explicit permission.

title: Efficient estimation: DR, DML, and TMLE
text:
Kennedy (2022), Semiparametric doubly robust targeted double machine learning: a review. Parts 1 to 3.





Edward Kennedy’s slides from the ACIC conference in 2022: Machine learning & nonparametric efficiency in causal inference. The slides present the same material as the paper, with some additional narrative and figures, and references throughout to relevant historical work. There are also some videos of Kennedy giving lectures on this topic on YouTube, for example here.
Hampel (1974), The influence curve and its role in robust estimation.
Lehmann and Casella (1999), Elements of Large-Sample Theory, especially Sections 1.1 to 1.4, 6.2 to 6.3, and 7.1 to 7.2.
crumbs: Week 7. Efficient estimation: DR, DML, and TMLE

title: PLSC 40601
text:
This is a graduate-level course considering modern advances in causal inference and experimental design. In particular, we will consider how machine learning methods can be leveraged to address causal questions. We will read a selection of papers introducing and implementing techniques and research designs, with applications to the social and health sciences and public policy.
We will discuss what these new methods are able to offer, and where they may have limitations. The course will be oriented around class discussion and student presentations on the readings.
An introductory course in probability and statistics is required; this prerequisite can be met by courses in statistics, biostatistics, economics, political science, sociology, or related fields. Coursework in causal inference is recommended but not required; additional reading references will be provided for students who have not had prior exposure to causal inference methodology.

title: PLSC 40601
text:
Schedule

Week 1. Causal inference, identification, and course orientation What changes once the causal question is already defined and machine learning is brought in as part of estimation, inference, and decision-making?
Week 2. Adjustment under selection on observables How should machine learning be used when identification rests on unconfoundedness and the core challenge is nuisance estimation under high-dimensional covariates?
Week 3. Heterogeneous treatment effects What kind of individualized causal object do you actually want to estimate: a CATE surface, a ranking over units, or an actionable rule?
Week 4. Representation learning for causal inference When is it useful to learn a representation of the covariates rather than estimate effects directly in the observed feature space?
Week 5. When ignorability fails: proxies and instruments What can you do once selection on observables is no longer plausible and you need either proxies for latent confounding or instruments for treatment variation?
Week 6. Policy learning and evaluation Once you can estimate heterogeneity, how do you turn it into treatment decisions and evaluate those decisions with observational or logged data?
Week 7. Efficient estimation: DR, DML, and TMLE How do influence functions, orthogonal scores, and cross-fitting organize the modern efficient-estimation toolbox?
Week 8. Generalizability, transportability, and policy shift Why does an internally valid causal estimate or learned policy not automatically transport to a new population, site, or deployment regime?
Week 9. AI-assisted measurement and valid inference How can model-generated labels improve measurement without being mistaken for ground truth?
